title: Mountain Range
description:
You are given n mountains in a row, each with a specific height. You may start your hang gliding route from any mountain.

You can glide from mountain a to mountain b if both conditions hold:
- height[a] > height[b]
- all mountains strictly between a and b have height < height[a]

What is the maximum number of mountains you can visit on your route?

Input
- First line: integer n
- Second line: n integers h_1, h_2, ..., h_n

Output
- A single integer: the maximum number of mountains you can visit.

examples.explanation: One optimal route is 40 → 25 → 19 → 13 → 12 (indices 6 → 5 → 8 → 9 → 10 in 1-based). Each glide goes from a higher to a lower mountain, and all mountains between are lower than the starting mountain of that glide.

mcqChain.questionText: Complete the glide rule: You can glide from mountain a to mountain b if height[a] > height[b] and all mountains between a and b ...?

options.label: …are lower than mountain a.
…are lower than both mountain a and b.
…form a strictly decreasing sequence.
…are lower than every mountain in the array.

mcqChain.explanation: The path is blocked if any intermediate height is ≥ height[a]. Only the start height matters for the in-between check. The destination must also be lower than a.

wrongExplanations.1: Requiring all between to be lower than both a and b is stricter than needed; only lower than a is required.
wrongExplanations.2: The sequence between a and b need not be decreasing; they just must be all < height[a].

mcqChain.hint: Think about what would block the glide from a: an intermediate mountain not lower than a.

mcqChain.questionText: For heights [7, 3, 5], what is the maximum number of mountains you can visit on a route?
mcqChain.explanation: Start at 7 → 5 (3 between is <7), then 5 → 3. Heights strictly decrease and in-between checks pass. So 3 mountains.

wrongExplanations.1: You can do at least 7→5; stopping at one is not optimal.
wrongExplanations.0: Routes longer than 3 aren't possible here; there are only 3 mountains.

mcqChain.hint: Try 7→5 and then 5→3. Check the in-between condition for each glide.

mcqChain.questionText: Along any valid route, how do the heights change?

options.label: Strictly decreasing
Non-increasing (equal allowed)
Arbitrary (can increase or decrease)
Strictly increasing

mcqChain.explanation: Each glide must go from a taller to a shorter mountain (height[a] > height[b]). Hence strictly decreasing.

wrongExplanations.1: Equal heights are not allowed in a glide; must be strictly lower.
wrongExplanations.2: Increases are never allowed by the rule.

mcqChain.hint: The rule requires height[a] > height[b] for each glide.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider heights [5, 5, 1]. Can you glide directly from the first 5 (index 1) to 1 (index 3)?

options.label: Yes, because 5 > 1 and 5 in the middle is not higher than 5.
No, because the middle 5 is not lower than the starting 5.
Yes, if you skip index 2 when checking.
No, because 1 is too far.

mcqChain.explanation: The middle 5 is equal to the start height, so it violates the requirement that all in-between heights must be < start height.

wrongExplanations.0: Equal is not lower; it blocks the glide.
wrongExplanations.2: You cannot skip intermediate mountains when checking the condition.

mcqChain.hint: Between a and b, all heights must be strictly less than height[a].

mcqChain.questionText: Why is a brute force enumeration of all possible routes infeasible for n up to 2⋅10^5?

options.label: It takes about O(n^2) time.
It can take exponential time in n.
It needs O(n log n) time.
It needs only O(n) time but lots of memory.

mcqChain.explanation: Routes correspond to many subsequences; trying all valid ones is exponential in the worst case.

wrongExplanations.0: Even checking all pairs is insufficient; enumerating routes is far more than n^2.
wrongExplanations.2: n log n suggests an efficient method, not brute force.

mcqChain.hint: How many decreasing subsequences could there be in worst case?

mcqChain.questionText: Fix a mountain i. To the right, how far can you glide before being blocked by taller or equal heights?
mcqChain.diagram:
… i … → → → …
Block occurs when you first meet height ≥ height[i].

options.label: Until the first mountain with height > height[i].
Until the first mountain with height ≥ height[i].
Until the first mountain with height ≥ height[i+1].
No blocking to the right; you can go to any lower mountain.

mcqChain.explanation: Any intermediate height ≥ height[i] blocks gliding past it. Strictly higher is not enough; equality also blocks.

wrongExplanations.0: Equality also blocks, not only strictly higher.
wrongExplanations.3: A taller or equal mountain in between blocks; you cannot go arbitrarily far.

mcqChain.hint: Remember the in-between requirement is strict: all must be < height[i].

mcqChain.questionText: Similarly on the left of i, which mountain blocks further gliding?

options.label: The nearest to the left with height ≥ height[i].
The nearest to the left with height > height[i].
The nearest to the left with height ≥ height[i−1].
None; only the right side matters.

mcqChain.explanation: Symmetric to the right: the first left mountain with height ≥ height[i] blocks.

wrongExplanations.1: Equal heights also block, not only strictly higher.

mcqChain.hint: Mirror the right-side reasoning for the left side.

mcqChain.questionText: Given the left/right blocking positions for i, which mountains are reachable from i in one glide?
mcqChain.diagram: … L | [ i … … ] | R …  where L and R are blockers (≥ height[i])

options.label: Any j strictly between L and R with height[j] < height[i].
Any j with height[j] < height[i] anywhere in the array.
Only immediate neighbors lower than i.
Any j between L and R, regardless of height.

mcqChain.explanation: Within (L,R), all in-between are < height[i], so any lower destination j inside is valid.

wrongExplanations.1: Being outside (L,R) introduces a blocking height ≥ height[i].
wrongExplanations.2: You are not limited to immediate neighbors.

mcqChain.hint: Combine the block boundaries with the strict height decrease requirement.

mcqChain.questionText: If each mountain (except the tallest in a region) attaches to a nearby taller blocker so that the entire order is preserved, what is true about the set of indices under any mountain in this hierarchy?
mcqChain.diagram: A mountain’s "region" is a contiguous block of indices where all heights are lower than it.

options.label: It forms a contiguous segment of indices with all heights strictly less than the mountain.
It is scattered across the array but all lower than the mountain.
It includes some higher heights as long as they are close.
It is always just one side (left only).

mcqChain.explanation: The blocker boundaries define a contiguous region where all heights are < the mountain’s height.

wrongExplanations.1: Blocker boundaries make the region contiguous, not scattered.
wrongExplanations.2: By definition, all inside must be strictly lower.

mcqChain.hint: Think of the first ≥ blockers on both sides defining a continuous interval.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider heights [20, 15, 17, 35, 25]. For index 3 (1-based, height 17), which index to the right first blocks gliding past it?

options.label: Index 4 (35)
Index 5 (25)
None, 17 is small
Index 3 itself

mcqChain.explanation: The first ≥17 to the right is 35 at index 4; it blocks gliding beyond.

wrongExplanations.1: 25 is ≥17 but it's not the first; 35 at 4 appears earlier.
wrongExplanations.2: There is a blocker at index 4.

mcqChain.hint: Scan rightward until you meet the first height ≥ 17.

mcqChain.questionText: With the same array, can you glide from index 3 (17) to index 5 (25)?

options.label: Yes, because 25 is within the region
No, because 17 is not taller than 25
Yes, because 35 blocks only beyond index 4
No, because 35 is between them

mcqChain.explanation: Even if the in-between were fine, height[3]=17 is not > 25. The glide must strictly decrease.

wrongExplanations.0: Being inside the region is not enough; destination must be lower.
wrongExplanations.3: 35 is between 3 and 5, but the stricter failure is 17<25.

mcqChain.hint: Both conditions must hold: in-between check and strict decrease.

mcqChain.questionText: From a mountain i, to maximize total visited mountains, is it enough to move first into the side (left or right) whose best chain is longer among its immediate children?
mcqChain.diagram:
i
├─ left child subtree
└─ right child subtree

options.label: Yes, because any longer chain to a deeper node starts with one of the children anyway.
No, you must always jump as far as possible.
No, you must alternate sides.
Yes, but only if both children are smaller than i.

mcqChain.explanation: Any descent path begins via a child. Stopping at the child cannot reduce the length versus skipping it.

wrongExplanations.1: Skipping intermediate lower nodes only reduces opportunities; it doesn’t help.
wrongExplanations.3: If a child is not lower, that side is blocked entirely anyway.

mcqChain.hint: You can always choose to stop at an intermediate lower node without blocking future moves.

mcqChain.questionText: If a child has the same height as its parent, can the parent glide into that child's side?

options.label: No, equal height blocks both landing on the child and gliding past it from the parent.
Yes, but only by skipping the child.
Yes, equal heights are allowed.
No, because the child is higher.

mcqChain.explanation: Equal height in between is not lower than the start, so it blocks passing, and landing on equal is forbidden.

wrongExplanations.1: You cannot skip a blocking equal height in-between.

mcqChain.hint: Revisit the equal-height blocking example [5,5,1].

mcqChain.questionText: Which statement gives the overall answer?

options.label: The maximum, over all starting mountains, of the best chain length reachable by descending to lower heights.
The best chain starting at the tallest mountain only.
The sum of best chains from left and right of the tallest.
The number of distinct heights.

mcqChain.explanation: You can start anywhere; take the maximum chain length among all starting points.

wrongExplanations.1: Equal-height blocks may make a non-tallest start optimal.
wrongExplanations.2: You cannot combine left and right in one route arbitrarily.

mcqChain.hint: You may start at any index; take the max over all.

mcqChain.questionText: Read input robustly and prepare heights into a list h (length n). Choose the correct code.

options.label:
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(1_000_000)
data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
if not data:
    print(0)
    raise SystemExit()
n = data[0]
h = data[1:1+n]

import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(1_000)
parts = sys.stdin.readline().split()
n = int(parts[0])
h = list(map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split()))

import sys
n = int(sys.stdin.read())
h = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))

import sys
data = sys.stdin.read().split()
h = list(map(int, data))
n = len(h)

mcqChain.explanation: Reading all ints then slicing ensures exactly n heights are used and works for one/two-line inputs. Also sets recursion limit safely.

wrongExplanations.1: Recursion limit may be too low; also fails if heights wrap to multiple lines or extra spaces.
wrongExplanations.3: This assumes all tokens are heights and sets n incorrectly.

mcqChain.hint: Prefer reading all integers and slicing based on the first value n.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(1_000_000)
data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
if not data:
    print(0)
    raise SystemExit()
n = data[0]
h = data[1:1+n]

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize helper arrays for building the hierarchy.

options.label:
left = [-1] * n
right = [-1] * n
parent = [-1] * n
stack = []

left = [0] * n
right = [0] * n
parent = [0] * n
stack = []

left = [None] * n
right = [None] * n
parent = [None] * n
stack = set()

left = []
right = []
parent = []
stack = []

mcqChain.explanation: -1 marks "no link" for indices. We'll use a list as a stack for indices.

wrongExplanations.1: 0 is a valid index; would confuse with first element.
wrongExplanations.3: These arrays need fixed size n for index access.

mcqChain.hint: Use -1 as a sentinel for missing links.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
left = [-1] * n
right = [-1] * n
parent = [-1] * n
stack = []

mcqChain.questionText: Build the hierarchy by scanning left-to-right, maintaining a non-increasing stack of indices. Choose the correct loop.
mcqChain.diagram: While current height is greater than the stack's top height, pop and attach as left child of current. If stack not empty, current becomes right child of stack top.
mcqChain.explanation: We pop strictly smaller heights (keep equals to block). Popped chain becomes left child of current. If a taller/equal remains, current is its right child.

wrongExplanations.1: Popping on <= breaks equal-height blocking. Left/right assignments are also swapped.
wrongExplanations.3: This ignores linking the popped node as a left child and loses structure.

mcqChain.hint: Equal heights must remain on stack to act as blockers; popped node attaches as left child of current.

mcqChain.questionText: Find the root index of the hierarchy.

options.label: root = stack[0]
root = right.index(-1)
root = n - 1

mcqChain.explanation: The root is the unique node with no parent (parent == -1).

wrongExplanations.1: While often true, relying on stack state can be fragile across implementations.
wrongExplanations.2: right == -1 indicates no right child, not the root.

mcqChain.hint: Which node never gets assigned a parent?

mcqChain.questionText: Build adjacency lists of children for each node.
mcqChain.explanation: Each node i has up to two children: left[i] and right[i]. Use independent lists to avoid aliasing.

wrongExplanations.1: Using [[]] * n aliases lists; also reverses parent-child direction.
wrongExplanations.2: This also works, but duplicates the child discovery; we already have left/right for each parent.

mcqChain.hint: Attach left[i] and right[i] as children of i if they exist.

mcqChain.questionText: Write a DFS that returns the best chain starting at u by only descending to strictly lower children, and also updates a global maximum.
mcqChain.diagram: best(u) = 1 + max(best(v) for children v with h[v] < h[u])

options.label:
ans = 0
def dfs(u):
    best = 1
    for v in children[u]:
        t = dfs(v)
        best = max(best, 1 + t)
    ans = max(ans, best)
    return best

mcqChain.explanation: Initialize best=1 (count u), only extend through children with h[v]<h[u], and update a global ans to cover all starting points.

wrongExplanations.1: Allows equal heights and loses the +1 for counting u.
wrongExplanations.3: Extends through all children even when not strictly lower; violates the rule.

mcqChain.hint: Include u itself, only extend to strictly lower children, and update a global maximum.

mcqChain.questionText: Finish by invoking the DFS from the root and printing the answer.

options.label:
dfs(root)
print(ans)
print(dfs(root))

dfs(0)
print(ans)

mcqChain.explanation: DFS from root visits all nodes (updating ans for every subtree), then print the global maximum.

wrongExplanations.1: dfs(root) returns best starting at root only; we need the global maximum across all starts.
wrongExplanations.3: Index 0 may not be the root and may miss parts of the structure.

mcqChain.hint: Your DFS updates a global maximum while visiting all nodes from the root.

mcqChain.questionText:
On the sample input:
10
20 15 17 35 25 40 12 19 13 12
what does the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: An optimal route is 40 → 25 → 19 → 13 → 12, so 5 mountains.

wrongExplanations.0: You can include more than 4 by stepping through intermediate allowed mountains.
wrongExplanations.2: You cannot get 6 because heights must strictly decrease and blockers exist.

mcqChain.hint: Try starting from the tallest (40) and descend through allowed lower mountains.

mcqChain.questionText: Test a case with duplicates: n=5, h=[5,5,4,4,3]. What is the maximum number of mountains you can visit?
mcqChain.explanation: Start at index 2 (5) → index 4 (4) → index 5 (3): 3 mountains. Equal heights block passing when necessary.

wrongExplanations.0: You can do 5→4→3 by choosing indices that avoid equal-height blockers.
wrongExplanations.2: Four is impossible due to equal-height blockers.

mcqChain.hint: Equal-height mountains block passing; choose a start that lets you reach 4 and then 3.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of this solution?
mcqChain.diagram: One pass to build structure + one pass DFS over n nodes

options.label: O(n)
O(n log n)

mcqChain.explanation: Each index is pushed/popped at most once in building, and DFS visits each node once.

wrongExplanations.1: There is no sorting or balanced tree; operations are linear.
wrongExplanations.2: We never consider all pairs; nested loops are avoided.

mcqChain.hint: Stack-based building and DFS both visit each element a constant number of times.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the space complexity (excluding input storage)?

options.label: O(n)
O(log n)

mcqChain.explanation: We store several arrays of length n and adjacency lists with O(n) total size.

wrongExplanations.2: We allocate arrays and a recursion stack; not constant.

mcqChain.hint: Count arrays: left, right, parent, children adjacency.

mcqChain.questionText: Edge cases: What is the answer when all heights are equal, and when heights are strictly monotonic (increasing or decreasing)?

options.label: All equal → 1; strictly monotonic → n
All equal → n; strictly monotonic → 1
All equal → 2; strictly monotonic → n/2
All equal → 1; strictly monotonic → floor(n/2)

mcqChain.explanation: Equal heights block any move, so 1. In strictly monotonic arrays, you can step down one by one to include all n.

wrongExplanations.1: Equal heights forbid any glide; you cannot visit more than one.
wrongExplanations.3: You can always chain all in monotonic cases.

mcqChain.hint: Try tiny examples like [5,5,5] and [1,2,3,4].

finalCodeSnippets:
import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(1_000_000)
data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
if not data:
    print(0)
    raise SystemExit()
n = data[0]
h = data[1:1+n]

left = [-1] * n
right = [-1] * n
parent = [-1] * n
stack = []

reasoningChain.question: What exactly blocks a glide between two mountains?
reasoningChain.insight: A glide from a to b is blocked if any intermediate mountain has height ≥ height[a]. Equality blocks.

reasoningChain.question: How far can a mountain "see" on either side?
reasoningChain.insight: Visibility extends until the first mountain on that side with height ≥ current height; within those bounds, any lower destination is reachable.

reasoningChain.question: How to model all possible glides compactly?
reasoningChain.insight: The nearest taller-or-equal blockers on both sides induce a rooted hierarchy where each node’s region is a contiguous index segment of strictly lower heights.

reasoningChain.question: What is the optimal next move from a mountain?
reasoningChain.insight: Any longer route going deeper starts via one of its two children; stopping at a lower child never reduces the route length.

reasoningChain.question: How to handle equal heights in the hierarchy?
reasoningChain.insight: Equal-height links exist but cannot be traversed by a glide; they block passing and cannot be part of a strictly decreasing route.

reasoningChain.question: How to compute the maximum route length efficiently?
reasoningChain.insight: Compute, for each node, 1 + max over strictly lower children’s best values; the answer is the maximum over all nodes, obtained during a single DFS.

reasoningChain.question: Can this be done in linear time and space?
reasoningChain.insight: Yes: build the hierarchy in O(n) with a single pass using a stack, then a single DFS in O(n), using O(n) space overall.
